Tools My Dashboard Resources FAQ Disclaimer Contact

Paid off medical collection but it's still on your credit report? New rules require removal. Learn how to dispute.

Paid Medical Collection Still on Credit Report: Should Be Removed

Understanding Your Situation

You paid off a medical collection account in full. You have proof of payment. But when you check your credit reports, the collection is still showing—possibly marked as 'paid collection' or still showing a balance. This should not be happening. Under new rules that took effect in July 2022 and updated in 2023, credit reporting agencies (Equifax, Experian, TransUnion) are required to remove paid medical collections from credit reports immediately upon payment. If a paid medical collection is still appearing on your credit report, the credit bureaus are violating federal Fair Credit Reporting Act (FCRA) standards and you have the right to demand immediate removal.

Why This Happens

Delayed Reporting Updates: Collection agencies may not promptly notify credit bureaus that the debt was paid, causing delays in removal.

Credit Bureau Processing Backlogs: Even after being notified, credit bureaus may take weeks or months to update reports, violating the prompt removal requirement.

Incorrect Status Updates: Instead of removing the account, bureaus may update it to 'paid' or '$0 balance' but leave it visible on your report.

Multiple Listings: The same medical debt may have been reported by different collectors or appear multiple times, and only some instances were removed.

System Errors: Credit bureau database errors may prevent proper removal even after the account should be deleted.

Lack of Awareness: Some collection agencies and credit bureaus haven't fully implemented the 2022-2023 policy changes requiring immediate removal of paid medical collections.

Waiting for 'Verification': Credit bureaus may improperly wait for verification before removing paid medical collections, when they should remove immediately upon learning of payment.

What To Check

✓ Confirm you paid the collection in full: Verify you have proof of payment (confirmation letter, receipt, bank statement)
✓ Check all three credit reports: Pull reports from Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion—is the account showing on one, two, or all three?
✓ Note how it's reported: Is it showing as 'paid,' '$0 balance,' 'settled,' or still showing balance owed?
✓ Verify payment date: How long ago did you pay? (Should be removed within days to weeks)
✓ Check for duplicate entries: Is the same debt listed multiple times or under different account numbers?
✓ Verify it's actually medical debt: Confirm the collection is specifically for medical services (medical debt has special protections)
✓ Review dispute history: Have you previously disputed this account with credit bureaus?
✓ Get free credit reports: Use AnnualCreditReport.com to get free reports from all three bureaus

What To Do Next

Step 1: Contact Collection Agency
Call the collection agency that reported the debt and verify:
• Confirm they received your payment in full
• Ask when they notified credit bureaus of payment and deletion
• Request written confirmation that the account was paid and should be deleted from credit reports
• Demand they send deletion requests to all three credit bureaus immediately

Get the name and direct contact info of the person you spoke with. Send follow-up via email and certified mail.

Step 2: Obtain Proof of Payment
Gather all documentation:
• Payment confirmation from collection agency
• Bank statements or canceled checks
• Receipts or payment portal screenshots
• Letter from collector confirming zero balance
• Any settlement agreement (if you settled for less than full amount)

Step 3: Dispute with All Three Credit Bureaus
File disputes with Equifax, Experian, and TransUnion citing the 2023 medical debt reporting rules:

'I am disputing this medical collection account. I paid this debt in full on [date] (proof of payment enclosed). Under the 2023 medical debt credit reporting rules implemented by the three major credit bureaus, paid medical collections must be removed from credit reports immediately upon payment. This account should not be appearing on my credit report. I demand immediate deletion of this account per industry standards and Fair Credit Reporting Act requirements. Continuing to report a paid medical collection violates FCRA § 1681s-2 accuracy requirements.'

Use our Medical Credit Report Removal tool to generate professional dispute letters for all three bureaus.

Step 4: Send Disputes via Certified Mail
Mail your disputes to:

Equifax:
Equifax Information Services LLC
P.O. Box 740256
Atlanta, GA 30374

Experian:
Experian
P.O. Box 4500
Allen, TX 75013

TransUnion:
TransUnion LLC
Consumer Dispute Center
P.O. Box 2000
Chester, PA 19016

Include copies (not originals) of payment proof and send via certified mail with return receipt.

Step 5: Follow Up After 30 Days
Credit bureaus must investigate and respond within 30 days. If they don't remove the account:
• Pull updated credit reports to verify status
• Send second dispute letter citing FCRA violations
• Escalate to bureau consumer affairs departments
• File complaints with Consumer Financial Protection Bureau

Step 6: File Regulatory Complaints
If credit bureaus refuse to remove paid medical collections:

• Consumer Financial Protection Bureau (CFPB): File CFPB Complaint
• Federal Trade Commission (FTC): Report to FTC
• Your State Attorney General: Consumer protection division

Specify that you're complaining about violation of medical debt reporting standards and FCRA accuracy requirements.

Step 7: Add Consumer Statement
While pursuing deletion, you can add a 100-word consumer statement to your credit file explaining the situation:

'This medical collection was paid in full on [date]. Under 2023 medical debt reporting rules, paid medical collections should be removed immediately. I am disputing this entry and have proof of payment.'

This statement appears to lenders who view your credit report.

Step 8: Consider Legal Action
If bureaus continue reporting paid medical collections after disputes and complaints, consult a consumer rights attorney specializing in FCRA violations:
• You can sue for inaccurate credit reporting under FCRA § 1681i and § 1681s-2
• Damages can include actual harm (denied credit, higher interest rates), statutory damages, and attorney fees
• Many attorneys take FCRA cases on contingency

For more guidance on credit reporting and medical debt, see our Medical Debt Hub.

Frequently Asked Questions

When did the rule change requiring removal of paid medical collections?

The three major credit bureaus implemented changes in July 2022 removing nearly 70% of medical collections from credit reports. In March 2023, they expanded protections to require immediate removal of ALL paid medical collections regardless of amount or date. These are industry policy changes implementing stricter standards than FCRA requires, but once implemented, bureaus must follow them.

What if I settled the debt for less than the full amount?

If you negotiated a settlement and paid the agreed amount in full, the collection should still be removed. 'Paid in full' for credit reporting purposes typically includes settled accounts. However, if the account shows 'settled' or 'settled for less,' specifically dispute citing the paid medical collection rule and provide proof of settlement completion.

How long after payment should collections be removed?

Credit bureaus should remove paid medical collections promptly—ideally within days to weeks. If it's been more than 30 days since payment and the account still appears, it's definitely time to dispute. There's no grace period allowing bureaus to delay removal.

Do I need to dispute with the collection agency or credit bureaus?

Both. Contact the collection agency to ensure they've notified credit bureaus of payment and deletion. Then dispute directly with all three credit bureaus. The bureaus are responsible for maintaining accurate reports, so even if the collector didn't notify them, you can compel removal through direct disputes.

What if the collection was from before 2023—does it still get removed?

Yes. The paid medical collection removal rule applies to ALL paid medical collections regardless of when the debt originated or when it was reported. If you paid a 10-year-old medical collection in 2024, it should be removed from your credit reports immediately upon payment.

Legal Disclaimer

Educational purposes only. This information is not legal, medical, or financial advice. Credit reporting laws and disputes are complex. Consult a consumer rights attorney or credit expert for personalized guidance.
